metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

coverage.verify_error: step 'review@then/verify_branch@then/verify' failed: the model hit the token cap before finishing (finish_reason=length) — its output is TRUNCATED; raise max_tokens or split the step

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the commit-backed fallback path fully: define the commit message/tree schema, how `read` retrieves and parses the blob from a commit object, and whether acquire/release CAS mechanics differ — so the session stays completable if AC0 returns a negative result.
findings.finding: If GitHub rejects blob refs (AC0 fallback), the plan says 'fall back to ref -> tiny-commit' but leaves acquire/release/read mechanics for a commit-backed ref entirely unspecified — the executor would need to design the commit schema, how `git cat-file` or `git show` retrieves the blob payload, and whether `git hash-object` is still used — making the session non-completable for the remaining ACs if the fallback fires.
findings.location: AC0 fallback clause / What section: 'If GitHub rejects it, fall back to ref -> tiny-commit and record that in the ADR'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a paragraph: 'If fallback to ref->commit is needed: create a commit with an empty tree and the JSON as the commit message (or a single-file tree); acquire CAS uses git update-ref <ref> <commit-oid> 0{40}; read uses git cat-file commit <ref> and parses the message body; release is git update-ref -d <ref> <commit-oid>. Blob hash-object step is omitted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `_is_cas_mismatch` generalization strategy: either 'parameterize with a `ref_name` argument, defaulting to `refs/heads/tickets` for backward compatibility' or 'extract a ref-agnostic variant and call it from the existing function' — pick one so the executor doesn't face a fork.
findings.finding: The plan presents two alternative generalizations for `_is_cas_mismatch` with an unresolved 'or' — 'generalized to accept the ref name (or a ref-agnostic variant is extracted)' — without specifying which approach to take, leaving a real design decision to the executor.
findings.location: What section: '_is_cas_mismatch (`:415`) is generalized to accept the ref name (or a ref-agnostic variant is extracted)'
findings.suggested_fix: Prefer: 'parameterize _is_cas_mismatch to accept an optional ref_name argument defaulting to refs/heads/tickets, preserving all existing call sites.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how AC0 is executed in CI: name the workflow file (e.g. `.github/workflows/spike-ref-lock.yml`), the trigger (e.g. `workflow_dispatch` or a dedicated CI step in the PR), and that its output (success/failure + refspec) is captured as a job artifact or ADR update — so the feasibility proof is reproducible, not just asserted.
findings.finding: AC0 specifies a 'proving command/test' but does not state how it is wired into CI — no workflow file, trigger, or job name is mentioned — so an executor could satisfy the letter of AC0 by writing a standalone script that is never run automatically, leaving the live feasibility unproven in CI.
findings.location: AC0 acceptance criterion: 'a proving command/test demonstrates refs/reconciler/* (ref -> blob) push+fetch through the origin GitHub remote with the CI token'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between plan body ('stale-oid release is a benign no-op success') and AC5 ('stale-oid release is rejected') — pick one authoritative contract and update both sections to match.
findings.finding: The plan body states a stale-oid release is a 'benign no-op success, not an error', while the AC states 'a stale-oid release is rejected' — these are direct contradictions that would send the implementer in two directions.
findings.location: Plan body ('Acquire vs release retry semantics differ' section) vs AC line 5 ('release requires the correct old oid and a stale-oid release is rejected')
findings.suggested_fix: The plan body's rationale (idempotent success) is the safer distributed-systems choice; update AC5 to read: 'a stale-oid release (ref already gone or owned by another) is treated as idempotent success, not an error'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC covering the structured log emission for acquire-failure and stale-oid release (e.g., assert log output contains expected fields in unit tests for those paths).
findings.finding: The plan body describes a deliverable — 'structured log emission for acquire-failure (already held) and release on a stale oid' — that has no covering acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Plan body (last bullet before 'Acceptance Criteria') vs Acceptance Criteria section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly document whether `_is_cas_mismatch` will keep backward compatibility for its existing callers (e.g., default `ref` parameter), and add at least a smoke-test or CI check that the existing advisory-lock path still classifies a refs/heads/tickets CAS mismatch correctly after the change.
findings.finding: The plan modifies `_is_cas_mismatch` in `_advisory_lock.py` (hard-coded to `refs/heads/tickets` at line 425) to accept a ref name, but does not acknowledge existing callers of this function, confirm backward compatibility, or provide a rollback path if the signature change breaks other consumers.
findings.location: Plan body ('Reuse the real CAS helper' section); _advisory_lock.py:415, 425
findings.suggested_fix: Use a default parameter: `def _is_cas_mismatch(stderr, ref='refs/heads/tickets')` so existing call sites are unaffected, then pass the ref explicitly from the new ref-lock code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify WHERE the promoted shared symbol will live (e.g., a new `_git_cas.py` utility module, or a clearly-demarcated public section of `_advisory_lock.py`) and note this interface boundary in the ADR so the coupling direction is explicit and maintainable.
findings.finding: The plan promotes a private helper (`_cas_advance_with_retry`, `_is_cas_mismatch`) to 'shared/semipublic symbol as needed' without specifying WHERE the shared symbol will live or what interface boundary separates `_ref_lock.py` from `_advisory_lock.py`. If `_ref_lock.py` simply imports directly from `_advisory_lock.py`, this creates a silent cross-module coupling with no defined interface layer — which risks entanglement as both modules evolve.
findings.location: What section: 'Promote the reused private helper to a shared/semipublic symbol as needed'
findings.suggested_fix: Extract `_cas_advance_with_retry` and the generalized `_is_cas_mismatch` into a new `src/rebar/_engine/_git_cas.py` (or similar) shared utility module that both `_advisory_lock.py` and `_ref_lock.py` import from. Document this module boundary in the ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Note in the plan (and ensure in implementation) that `_is_cas_mismatch`'s docstring/inline comment is updated to reflect its newly generalized ref-name parameter, and that any existing developer docs referencing its tickets-specific behavior are corrected.
findings.finding: The plan modifies `_is_cas_mismatch` (which currently hard-codes `refs/heads/tickets` at line 425) to accept a generic ref name, but does not mention updating any existing documentation, inline docstrings, or CLAUDE.md entries that describe the current behavior of `_is_cas_mismatch` as tickets-specific. The existing contract becomes stale without a noted update path.
findings.location: _advisory_lock.py:415-425 / What section: '_is_cas_mismatch... is generalized to accept the ref name'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a wall-clock timeout (e.g. via `subprocess.run(..., timeout=N)`) for all git subprocess calls in `_ref_lock.py`, and define what `acquire`/`release` raise on timeout so callers can apply a circuit-breaker or fallback.
findings.finding: No timeout is specified for git subprocess calls (git hash-object, git update-ref, git push/fetch to the GitHub remote). If the remote is slow or unavailable, the acquire/release operation blocks indefinitely, stalling whatever calls the lock primitive.
findings.location: What section — blob creation / CAS acquire/release path; AC0 remote push+fetch
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap all `subprocess.run` / `Popen` calls in `_ref_lock.py` with `timeout=` (e.g. 30 s for remote ops, 5 s for local object ops) and raise a distinct `RefLockTimeoutError`; document the timeout values in the ADR alongside the CAS contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document a break-glass procedure (e.g. `git push origin :refs/reconciler/lock`) in the ADR for manually clearing a leaked lock in C1 before C2 steal logic is available, so operators have a recovery path if a holder crashes.
findings.finding: The plan defers all crash-recovery / lock-expiry logic to C2 with no operator break-glass path defined for C1. If a holder crashes while holding the lock in a C1 deployment, the ref persists and no new acquire can succeed — with no documented manual remediation.
findings.location: What section — 'No lease-expiry/steal logic here (that is C2)'; AC acceptance criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Operator runbook' subsection to the new ADR entry describing the manual `git push origin :refs/reconciler/lock` (or `git update-ref -d`) procedure and when it is safe to use (i.e. confirmed holder is dead).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve and state explicitly whether `_is_cas_mismatch` is modified in place (requiring the existing `refs/heads/tickets` call site at `_advisory_lock.py:425` to be updated) or a new ref-agnostic variant is extracted (leaving the existing caller untouched); document the chosen approach in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan says `_is_cas_mismatch` will be 'generalized to accept the ref name (or a ref-agnostic variant is extracted)' — the 'or' leaves unresolved whether the existing function's signature is modified in place or a new variant is extracted. If the existing function is changed in place, the current caller at `_advisory_lock.py:425` (which hard-codes `refs/heads/tickets`) is a dependent whose behavior will change and must be explicitly acknowledged/updated.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Reuse the real CAS helper' section; `_advisory_lock.py:415,425`
findings.suggested_fix: Prefer extracting a new variant (e.g., `_is_cas_mismatch_for_ref(ref: str)`) and keeping the existing call site unchanged, then note in the plan: 'The existing `_is_cas_mismatch` call in `_advisory_lock.py:425` is unchanged; the new variant is used only in `_ref_lock.py`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or update tests for `_is_cas_mismatch` (and/or `_cas_advance_with_retry`) verifying that the generalized discriminator still correctly classifies CAS mismatches for the original `refs/heads/tickets` ref after the hard-coded string is removed.
findings.finding: The plan generalizes `_is_cas_mismatch` (`_advisory_lock.py:415`, previously hard-coding `refs/heads/tickets` at line 425) to accept a configurable ref name, which changes the behavior of existing code. No test work is specified for verifying that existing `_advisory_lock.py` callers (which depend on the old hard-coded discriminator behavior) continue to function correctly after the refactor — a changed behavior with no corresponding modify/update-test work.
findings.location: AC bullet: 'The CAS reuses `_cas_advance_with_retry` … `_is_cas_mismatch` (`:415`) is generalized off the hard-coded `refs/heads/tickets`'
findings.suggested_fix: In the unit-test AC bullet, add: 'regression test that `_is_cas_mismatch` (or its successor) correctly classifies exit-128 for `refs/heads/tickets` unchanged after generalization, and correctly classifies exit-128 for `refs/reconciler/lock`.' A parametrized test over both ref names covering the mismatch and non-mismatch cases is sufficient.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `fence` and `heartbeat_ns` are genuinely required by a C1 AC; if they serve only C2 (steal/expiry), defer them to the C2 ticket and trim the C1 blob schema to `{holder, lease_secs}` only.
findings.finding: `fence` and `heartbeat_ns` blob fields have no C1 AC consumer — they are pre-designed for C2 (steal-CAS / lease-expiry). Including them in the C1 schema is YAGNI: the AC unit-test list does not verify or even read these fields, and the plan explicitly states 'No lease-expiry/steal logic here (that is C2)'.
findings.location: What section — blob schema: `{holder, lease_secs, heartbeat_ns, fence}`; AC unit-test list
findings.suggested_fix: Reduce the C1 blob schema to `{holder, lease_secs}` (what C1 actually uses). Add `heartbeat_ns` and `fence` in the C2 ticket where steal/expiry logic is defined and can be tested end-to-end.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify promoting `_cas_advance_with_retry` / `_is_cas_mismatch` to shared/semipublic status with only two consumers, OR keep the symbols private to `_advisory_lock.py` and let `_ref_lock.py` call them directly (same module package) without a formal promotion.
findings.finding: The plan promotes `_cas_advance_with_retry` and `_is_cas_mismatch` from private (`_advisory_lock.py`) to 'shared/semipublic' symbols, but each currently has exactly one call-site in the codebase (`_advisory_lock.py:469` and `:585`). Adding `_ref_lock.py` yields two call-sites — still below the Rule-of-Three threshold for a formal shared abstraction. The promotion is premature unless the plan articulates why two sites justify the API surface.
findings.location: What section — 'Reuse the real CAS helper … Promote the reused private helper to a shared/semipublic symbol as needed'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `_advisory_lock.py:469` (the `_is_cas_mismatch(exc)` call inside `_cas_advance_with_retry`) and the `_cas_advance_with_retry` signature (line 447) as explicit edit targets required to thread the ref-name parameter through to the generalized discriminator.
findings.finding: The plan says to generalize `_is_cas_mismatch` (line 415) 'to accept the ref name', but the ONLY internal call site that must be updated — `_cas_advance_with_retry` line 469 (`if not _is_cas_mismatch(exc)`) — is not enumerated as an edit target. The plan does not specify how `_cas_advance_with_retry` itself receives/forwards the ref name to the generalized `_is_cas_mismatch`; without updating both the signature of `_cas_advance_with_retry` AND its body at line 469, the generalization is incomplete.
findings.location: What section — 'Reuse the real CAS helper' paragraph; `_advisory_lock.py:469`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `ref_name: str` parameter to `_cas_advance_with_retry` (and update its single existing call at line 585 to pass `'refs/heads/tickets'`), then update line 469 to call `_is_cas_mismatch(exc, ref_name=ref_name)`. List both as explicit edit targets in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_advisory_lock.py:585` (the sole existing caller of `_cas_advance_with_retry`) to the plan's enumerated edit targets, noting it must be updated to pass `ref_name='refs/heads/tickets'` when the signature is extended.
findings.finding: The plan does not enumerate the existing call site at `_advisory_lock.py:585` (`_cas_advance_with_retry(repo_root, _mutate_and_advance)`) as a required update. If `_cas_advance_with_retry`'s signature is extended to accept a ref name (needed to support the generalized `_is_cas_mismatch`), the existing call at line 585 must be updated to pass `'refs/heads/tickets'`, or it will break at runtime.
findings.location: What section — 'Reuse the real CAS helper' paragraph; `_advisory_lock.py:585`
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's edit target list, explicitly include: update line 585 from `_cas_advance_with_retry(repo_root, _mutate_and_advance)` to `_cas_advance_with_retry(repo_root, _mutate_and_advance, ref_name='refs/heads/tickets')`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `git push origin refs/reconciler/lock:<new-oid>` step (and a matching `git fetch origin refs/reconciler/lock:refs/reconciler/lock` before read) as explicit parts of the acquire/release/read mechanism — the remote push IS the distributed CAS, not a follow-on step.
findings.finding: The acquire/release mechanism as specified in the plan body is entirely local (`git hash-object -w` + `git update-ref`) with no `git push` to origin. For distributed locking across CI runners — the stated problem — the ref must be authoritative on the GitHub remote; a local-only CAS provides zero cross-runner exclusion. The AC0 spike proves push+fetch is feasible, but the acquire/release mechanism never names a push step, leaving a critical hole in the distributed-lock protocol.
findings.location: What section: 'Blob creation: git hash-object -w --stdin plants the blob … THEN git update-ref <ref> <oid> <old>: create-only CAS acquire'; AC line: 'acquire is create-only CAS (old=zeros), release is observed-oid CAS; blob is created via git hash-object -w before update-ref'
findings.suggested_fix: Reframe the acquire mechanism as: (1) `git fetch origin refs/reconciler/lock:refs/reconciler/lock` to see current remote state, (2) `git hash-object -w --stdin` to plant the blob locally, (3) `git push origin <blob-oid>:refs/reconciler/lock` with `--force-with-lease=refs/reconciler/lock:<observed-oid>` (or `0000...` for first acquire) as the actual distributed CAS. The remote push is the atomic gate, not the local `update-ref`. Local `update-ref` is an optional mirror step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete interface extension to `_cas_advance_with_retry` needed to support acquire/release/steal semantics — e.g., add an `on_mismatch` parameter (enum: RETRY | STOP_FAIL | STOP_SUCCESS) or extract a lower-level `_cas_once` helper that the new paths call directly without the retry loop.
findings.finding: `_cas_advance_with_retry` unconditionally retries every CAS exit-128 up to `_CAS_RETRY_BUDGET` (its semantics are 'rebuild the commit on the new tip and try again'). The plan directs reusing this function for three paths with incompatible exit-128 semantics: acquire exit-128 must NOT retry (means lock held — stop immediately), release exit-128 must be a silent success (not retried or raised), and steal exit-128 (C2) is the 'loser stops' path. The plan acknowledges this ('the reused retry loop must be told which path it is on') but specifies no interface change to the function, leaving the implementer without a concrete extension plan.
findings.location: What section: 'Reuse the real CAS helper _cas_advance_with_retry … Acquire vs release retry semantics differ (so the reused retry loop must be told which path it is on)'; _advisory_lock.py:447-487
findings.suggested_fix: Extract a `_cas_once(repo_root, mutate_and_advance) -> bool` (returns True on success, False on CAS mismatch, raises on other errors). Acquire calls it directly and maps False → 'lock held'. Release wraps it and maps both True and False → success. `_cas_advance_with_retry` is reimplemented as `_cas_once` in a retry loop — preserving existing behavior without duplication.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale explaining why the chosen CAS primitive (local `update-ref` + remote push vs. `push --force-with-lease` directly) was selected, so the implementer understands the intended distributed CAS contract.
findings.finding: The plan states a positive rationale for ref→blob over working-tree file (avoids union-merge, never in working tree) and for `time.time_ns()` over HLC, but gives no positive rationale for the two-step local-CAS-then-(implicit?)-push sequence vs. using `git push --force-with-lease=<ref>:<old>` directly to origin as the single-step distributed CAS primitive. The latter would collapse acquire into one network round-trip and is the established Git idiom for remote CAS, making the absence of rationale for the chosen two-step approach a plan gap.
findings.location: What section: 'Blob creation' and 'Reuse the real CAS helper'; also AC line: 'acquire is create-only CAS (old=zeros)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a section to the ADR (or the 'What' rationale) explaining specifically what the existing `concurrency: group: reconcile-bridge, cancel-in-progress: false` (reconcile-bridge.yml:51-53) fails to cover that the new bare-ref CAS lock addresses, justifying the novel architecture against this established prior art.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel remote-ref CAS lock architecture without comparing it against the GitHub Actions `concurrency:` group already deployed in `reconcile-bridge.yml` (which already serializes reconciler runs at the workflow level with `cancel-in-progress: false`). No rationale explains why the existing workflow-level serialization is insufficient, making the novel architecture unjustified against this established, zero-cost prior art.
findings.location: ## What — 'bare-ref CAS lock primitive' / AC0
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR and/or plan 'What' section, state the gap: e.g. 'GitHub Actions concurrency groups only serialize within one repo's workflow runs; a multi-clone or cross-repo scenario, or a manually-invoked pass, bypasses them — the ref lock provides a cross-invocation guard.' If no such gap exists, reconsider whether the ref lock is needed at all.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC0's scope to explicitly verify that `contents:write` (the standard CI token) is sufficient to push to `refs/reconciler/*` (not just that GitHub accepts the blob object type), and document the permission requirement in the ADR alongside the refspec.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the CI `GITHUB_TOKEN` with `contents:write` permission is sufficient to push `refs/reconciler/*` to the GitHub remote. This is established for `refs/heads/*` (docs/jira-sync-setup.md:223 documents `contents: write` covers ticket-branch pushes), but GitHub's permission model for non-standard ref namespaces (`refs/reconciler/*`) is not documented in the codebase and has not been verified as equivalent.
findings.location: ## What — AC0: '…using the CI `GITHUB_TOKEN` (contents:write)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require the new ADR entry to document: (a) why ref→blob is preferred over ref→tiny-commit and over git-notes (`refs/notes/*`) semantics, (b) fully specify the tiny-commit fallback's acquire/release/read contract so AC0's fallback path is implementable without a follow-on design ticket.
findings.finding: The plan commits to ref→blob as the primary design over the well-established ref→tiny-commit alternative without documenting the justification for that choice. The fallback to ref→tiny-commit is mentioned ('fall back to ref -> tiny-commit') but its acquire/release/read mechanics are entirely unspecified. `git notes` refs (`refs/notes/*`) are a well-known prior art for non-branch ref storage with established GitHub remote round-trip semantics that the plan does not consider.
findings.location: ## What — Blob creation paragraph / AC0 fallback

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `git fetch` invocation (e.g. `git fetch origin refs/reconciler/lock:refs/reconciler/lock`) that `read` (and the acquire path) must execute before consulting the local ref, and add a corresponding refspec to the CI workflow or document it as a required `_ref_lock` implementation detail in the ADR.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies how `refs/reconciler/*` is fetched from origin before `read` is called on a contending runner. The current CI workflow only fetches `+tickets:refs/remotes/origin/tickets` (reconcile-bridge.yml:102); there is no configured fetch refspec for `refs/reconciler/*` anywhere in the codebase. Without an explicit `git fetch origin refs/reconciler/lock:refs/reconciler/lock` (or equivalent), `read` sees only the local ref state — which may be absent or stale — making the distributed CAS acquire semantically equivalent to a local lock, defeating the remote coordination purpose.
findings.location: What / AC0 / module design (`acquire`/`release`/`read`) — no fetch refspec specified
findings.suggested_fix: In the `read` implementation, always run `git fetch origin refs/reconciler/lock:refs/reconciler/lock` (non-force, single-ref) before reading the local ref value; document this as the canonical 'sync from remote' step. Add the same fetch before the acquire CAS. Add `git fetch origin refs/reconciler/*:refs/reconciler/*` to the workflow's checkout step (or as a `_ref_lock` internal step) so the ref namespace is always current.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC0 to explicitly verify that `GITHUB_TOKEN` with only `contents: write` can push to and delete `refs/reconciler/*` (not just `refs/heads/*`), and record the verified minimum permission in the ADR.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `contents:write` GITHUB_TOKEN permission is sufficient to push to the custom `refs/reconciler/*` namespace on GitHub, but does not require AC0 to explicitly verify and document this permission boundary. GitHub's `contents:write` scope covers 'repository contents, commits, branches, downloads, releases, and merges' — its applicability to arbitrary non-branch, non-tag custom ref namespaces (refs/reconciler/*) is UNVERIFIED against GitHub's hosted-repo permission enforcement.
findings.location: AC0 / What section (GITHUB_TOKEN contents:write) / Acceptance Criteria AC0

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require AC0 to explicitly confirm and document in the ADR that `contents:write` alone is sufficient for pushing `refs/reconciler/*` to the GitHub remote; if a broader grant is required, record the justification and minimum-necessary scope in both the ADR and the workflow `permissions:` block.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `contents:write` is the sufficient GITHUB_TOKEN grant for pushing `refs/reconciler/*` to the GitHub remote, but does not require AC0 to explicitly verify and document minimum token scope. If GitHub's permission model requires a broader grant (e.g., a PAT or `administration:write`) for custom non-branch ref namespaces, that undeclared escalation would violate least-privilege without being caught by the AC0 feasibility spike as written.
findings.location: AC0 section — 'CI `GITHUB_TOKEN` (contents:write)'; Acceptance Criteria AC0 bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC0 acceptance criterion: 'The minimum GITHUB_TOKEN permission required for push+fetch of refs/reconciler/* is confirmed (contents:write or documented alternative) and recorded in the ADR.' Add a parallel fallback note: 'If a broader scope is required, the ADR records the security decision and the workflow permissions: block is updated to the confirmed minimum, no wider.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `RefLockCorruptError` fully in the plan: its module location, what conditions trigger it (JSON decode failure, missing required fields, wrong field types, empty blob), and what payload (if any) it carries — so the fail-closed contract in `read` is unambiguously implementable.
findings.finding: `RefLockCorruptError` is a named exception type the plan says `read` raises and callers must handle as HELD, but it does not exist anywhere in the codebase and its definition — including what conditions trigger it (corrupt JSON, partial write, empty blob, missing fields, wrong types) — is never specified in the plan.
findings.location: What section — 'Decode of a corrupt/partial/empty blob fails closed: read raises a named RefLockCorruptError'; AC line: 'read of a corrupt/empty blob raises and callers treat it as HELD'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the schema section: '`RefLockCorruptError(ValueError)` defined in `_ref_lock.py`; raised by `read` on any of: (a) empty/non-UTF-8 bytes, (b) invalid JSON, (c) missing any of {holder, lease_secs, heartbeat_ns, fence}, (d) fence not a non-negative int. Carries `raw: bytes` for debugging.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate a per-operation fence rule table with the blob schema definition: specify that C1 `acquire` writes `fence=0`, C1 `release` does not read/write fence (deletes the ref), C1 `read` returns fence as int, and C2 `renew`/`steal` increments fence — so the counter placement is unambiguous for both C1 and C2 implementors.
findings.finding: The `fence` counter's seed value and C1-operation update rule are not co-located with the blob schema definition. The plan says 'seeded to 0 on first acquire; C2 increments it on renew/steal' but never states what `release` does to `fence` in the blob it reads/validates, nor whether `acquire` writes `fence=0` unconditionally or only when no prior blob exists.
findings.location: What section — 'fence is a monotonic progress-witness / generation counter (seeded to 0 on first acquire; C2 increments it on renew/steal)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete API change that makes `_cas_advance_with_retry` express three distinct exit-128 policies (acquire=stop, release=idempotent-success, only genuine races retry) — e.g. a `on_cas_mismatch: Callable[[CalledProcessError], Literal['stop','success','retry']]` callback — co-located with the reuse directive.
findings.finding: The plan directs reuse of `_cas_advance_with_retry` for acquire, release, and steal, but that helper's loop (confirmed lines 464-487) has a single hardwired retry policy: retry-on-mismatch up to `_CAS_RETRY_BUDGET`. The plan requires three distinct exit-128 outcomes (acquire=stop/held, release=idempotent-success, steal=stop/lost), yet provides no specification of how the retry loop is parameterized or overridden to express these protocols — the decision rule is not co-located with the reuse directive.
findings.location: What section — 'Reuse the real CAS helper _cas_advance_with_retry (_advisory_lock.py:447-487)'; 'Acquire vs release retry semantics differ (so the reused retry loop must be told which path it is on)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either specify the commit-backed fallback's acquire/release/read mechanics in the What section (e.g. 'JSON payload is the sole file in the commit tree; CAS old= is the commit OID; same blob schema applies'), or explicitly declare the fallback out-of-scope for this ticket and require a new ticket if AC0 triggers it.
findings.finding: The AC0 fallback ('if GitHub rejects blob refs, fall back to ref → tiny-commit and record that in the ADR') is named but its mechanics are entirely unspecified: no acquire/release/read semantics for the commit-backed variant, no blob-vs-commit branching in `_ref_lock.py`, and all subsequent ACs (AC1–AC4) are written exclusively for the blob path. The fallback sub-step has no defined processing protocol.
findings.location: AC0: 'the working refspec (and any ref->commit fallback, which the blob-carrying scheme adapts to) is documented in the ADR'; What section: 'If GitHub rejects it, fall back to ref -> tiny-commit and record that in the ADR'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
